        if (s === "contactability") return b.scores.contactability - a.scores.contactability;
        if (s === "completeness") return b.scores.completeness - a.scores.completeness;
        return a.name.localeCompare(b.name);
    }

I read "'`paradisio_app/static/styles.css` and `paradisio_app/static/app.js`, then inspected the mobile screenshots under `docs/paradisio_app/screenshots/mobile/`. Note: the directory contains 20 PNGs, not 16, because the filtered homepage state is captured as both `filter_restaurant` and `filter_whatsapp` variants across the four devices.

Overall assessment:
The UI is clean, readable, and touch-friendly in the basic sense. Text contrast is strong, the off-white background gives the directory a calmer feel than a plain white list, selects/search inputs are comfortably tappable, and cards scan reasonably well. The main mobile problem is vertical economy: the header, stats, search, and four full-width selects take most of the first viewport before the user sees meaningful results, especially on iPhone SE. The second problem is hierarchy: result cards give nearly equal weight to name, badges, scores, and CTA, so the eye has to work harder than it should. The third problem is the business detail sticky CTA: it is useful, but on smaller captures it overlaps the bottom content/back link and needs more safe-area-aware spacing.

View-by-view critique:

1. Homepage default state
- iPhone SE 375x667: The page is readable, but the first result starts only after a large control block. Four stacked selects plus the search field consume too much of the first screen. Result card text is legible, but long business names wrap aggressively and push badges/scores/actions down. Badges are clear but visually numerous.
- iPhone 12 390x844 and Pixel 5 393x851: Same structure works better because the taller viewport shows more of the first and second card. The density is still high, but less cramped than SE. Width differences between iPhone 12 and Pixel 5 are negligible.
- iPhone Pro Max 430x932: The page breathes better, but the layout does not take advantage of the extra width. Cards still use the same large vertical rhythm; controls remain a long form rather than a compact filter surface.
- What works: strong title, obvious search, 44px+ controls, clear result count, cards are easy to tap.
- What breaks: too much setup before results, no compact filter summary/collapse, full-page renders are enormous for default/WhatsApp states because hundreds of cards are in DOM.

2. Homepage with search query (`cacao`)
- The narrowed result count is useful and visible. Search state is obvious because the query remains in the input.
- The same control stack remains present even though the user has already narrowed results; this makes search feel slower than necessary on SE because the first result still sits low on the screen.
- Long result names such as location-heavy entries dominate cards. The primary action is visually below several metadata rows, so it is not the immediate next action.
- Recommendation: when search has a value, make filters collapsible or show a single `Filters` button plus active chips; keep the result count close to the input.

3. Homepage with WhatsApp filter
- The selected state is technically visible through the select label (`Has WhatsApp`), but it reads like another form value rather than an active filter. There is no clear/reset affordance.
- Because this still returns 614 results, it is almost equivalent to default but with one select changed. Users may not feel that the filter meaningfully narrowed the list.
- The default alphabetical sort puts less relevant-looking entries first even when filtering by contact channel. For contact-channel flows, sorting by contactability or completeness would better match intent.

4. Homepage with restaurant + WhatsApp filters
- This is the strongest list state: 98 results feels meaningfully narrowed, and the first cards are relevant.
- On SE, the first card still starts below the fold-heavy control block, but the result quality makes the interaction clearer.
- The active filters are buried inside selects. A compact row like `Restaurant x`, `WhatsApp x`, `Reset` would make state and reversibility much clearer.

5. Business detail page
- The top hierarchy is mostly good: back link, business name, category/area/status, badges, action links, map, scores, description, claim section.
- The three secondary action buttons (`Call`, `Instagram`, `Google Maps`) are large enough, but their visual weight competes with the sticky CTA. The page has both top actions and bottom actions; this is useful, but redundant.
- The map is prominent and legible. On larger mobile sizes it feels balanced; on SE it takes a large chunk of the viewport but is still acceptable for a local directory.
- Score bars are readable, but the labels are long and right-aligned. On narrow width this creates a slightly technical/dashboard feel; users may not know why `Contactability`, `Visibility`, and `Completeness` matter.
- Sticky bar: strong primary action and good tap targets. However, on iPhone SE the sticky bar overlaps the bottom region and leaves a repeated back link partially awkward beneath it. On Pro Max the sticky bar sits after a large blank region in the full-page capture, which suggests the content height/padding is not tuned to the viewport.

Top 3 fixes:

1. Compress and clarify the mobile filter area.
Use a compact filter drawer or 2-column grid below 390px+ rather than four full-width selects. Keep search full-width, then show active filter chips and a single `Filters` control. This would bring results into view much earlier.

Suggested CSS/HTML direction:
```css
@media (max-width: 639px) {
  .header { padding: 14px 0 8px; }
  .header h1 { font-size: 1.45rem; }
  .stats-bar { gap: 2px 10px; font-size: 0.72rem; }
  .search-input { padding: 12px 14px; border-radius: 9px; }
  .filters { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 8px; }
  .filter-select { min-height: 44px; padding: 10px; font-size: 0.85rem; }
}
@media (max-width: 380px) {
  .filters { grid-template-columns: 1fr; }
}
```
Better HTML/JS improvement: render active filters as chips above results and add a reset button.

2. Make result cards more scannable and action-led.
Clamp long names, reduce badge dominance, group scores into one muted line or hide scores behind a compact indicator. Make the primary contact action a button-like row only when it is the actual recommended action.

Suggested CSS:
```css
.result-card { padding: 12px; border-radius: 8px; }
.result-name {
  font-size: 0.98rem;
  line-height: 1.25;
  display: -webkit-box;
  -webkit-line-clamp: 2;
  -webkit-box-orient: vertical;
  overflow: hidden;
}
.result-meta { font-size: 0.76rem; gap: 3px 8px; }
.result-channels { gap: 4px; margin-top: 6px; }
.channel-tag { font-size: 0.64rem; padding: 2px 6px; }
.result-scores { color: #888; font-size: 0.68rem; }
.result-cta { font-size: 0.86rem; padding-top: 2px; }
```
Also consider stripping location suffixes from display names when category/area already show location.

3. Fix sticky bottom CTA spacing and detail-page bottom layout.
The sticky CTA is valuable, but it needs safe-area padding and enough content padding so it never covers footer/back content. The duplicate bottom back link should either sit above the sticky-safe area or be removed on mobile.

Suggested CSS:
```css
.biz-content {
  padding-bottom: calc(112px + env(safe-area-inset-bottom));
}
.sticky-bar {
  padding: 10px 16px calc(10px + env(safe-area-inset-bottom));
  min-height: 76px;
}
.sticky-bar .primary-cta { padding: 13px 16px; font-size: 1rem; }
```
